seo.title: AutoGen Guardrails for Healthcare
seo.description: Compliance guide for AutoGen agents in healthcare. Meet HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Healthcare teams using AutoGen for multi-agent systems face heightened risks due to the framework's conversational nature, which can lead to infinite loops, unsafe code execution, and unverified medical conclusions. These failure modes threaten patient safety and regulatory compliance in handling PHI under HIPAA and FDA rules. Vex provides essential guardrails by filtering agent replies in real-time, ensuring auditable, secure, and compliant AI-assisted decisions.

complianceRequirements.description: Protects PHI from unauthorized disclosure; requires de-identification before LLM processing and prevents data from leaving secure environments.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex filters agent replies to block PHI leakage, enforces de-identification patterns, and logs all interactions for audit trails.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates electronic records and signatures be trustworthy, reliable, and auditable for AI-assisted medical decisions.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex registers as a reply filter to validate and timestamp agent outputs, ensuring tamper-proof audit logs for regulatory validation.

complianceRequirements.description: Strengthens HIPAA with breach notifications and enforces strict data handling for healthcare AI systems.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex detects and prevents breaches in multi-agent conversations, alerting on potential PHI exposure in real-time.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires controls for security, availability, and confidentiality in healthcare data processing platforms.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex monitors token usage and conversation flows to prevent uncontrolled spending and ensures secure, available agent interactions.

industryRisks.name: Infinite conversation loops exposing PHI
industryRisks.description: Agents endlessly debate without termination, repeatedly processing sensitive patient data.
industryRisks.example: Diagnostic agents loop on a patient's MRI results, leaking PHI via repeated LLM calls.

industryRisks.name: Unsafe code execution in medical tooling
industryRisks.description: Agents generate and execute code that mishandles patient data or produces harmful recommendations.
industryRisks.example: Agent executes unverified Python to analyze bloodwork, outputting incorrect dosage calculations.

industryRisks.name: Agents converging on incorrect diagnoses
industryRisks.description: Multi-agent consensus leads to flawed medical conclusions without human oversight.
industryRisks.example: Agents agree on misdiagnosing a rare condition based on incomplete symptoms.

industryRisks.name: Uncontrolled token spending on PHI debates
industryRisks.description: Extended multi-turn discussions inflate costs and risk prolonged PHI exposure.
industryRisks.example: Debate on treatment plans consumes excessive tokens while PHI remains in context.

implementation.title: Register Vex as AutoGen Reply Filter
implementation.description: Install Vex and configure it as a reply function filter to scan all agent responses for compliance.
implementation.code:
import autogen

from vex_sdk import VexClient

# Initialize Vex client with healthcare policy
vex = VexClient(api_key="your-vex-key", policy="healthcare-hipaa")

# Define Vex reply filter
def vex_filter(messages, sender, config):
    response = vex.scan(" ".join([m.content for m in messages]))
    if not response.compliant:
        return "Blocked: PHI detected or non-compliant output."
    return None  # Allow reply

# Configure AutoGen agent with Vex filter
config_list = [{"model": "gpt-4", "api_key": "openai-key"}]

llm_config = {"config_list": config_list, "reply_filter": [vex_filter]}

agent = autogen.AssistantAgent(name="healthcare_agent", llm_config=llm_config)

implementation.title: Prevent Infinite Loops with Vex Termination
implementation.description: Use Vex to detect and terminate looping conversations, enforcing max turns for audits.

implementation.title: Audit AI Decisions with Vex Logging
implementation.description: Log all agent decisions with Vex for HIPAA/FDA auditability, including PHI de-identification checks.
implementation.code:
from vex_sdk import VexClient
import autogen

vex = VexClient(api_key="your-vex-key", policy="fda-part11")

def audit_filter(messages, sender, config):
    content = " ".join([m.content for m in messages])
    # Check for PHI and de-identify
    audit_result = vex.audit(content, context="medical_decision", tags=["PHI", "diagnosis"])
    if audit_result.phi_detected and not audit_result.deidentified:
        return "BLOCKED: PHI must be de-identified before processing."
    # Log for audit
    vex.log_decision({
        "agent": sender.name,
        "decision": content,
        "compliant": audit_result.compliant,
        "timestamp": audit_result.timestamp
    })
    return None

llm_config = {"config_list": config_list, "reply_filter": [audit_filter]}
clinical_agent = autogen.AssistantAgent(name="clinician", llm_config=llm_config)

caseStudyScenario.heading: AutoGen in Healthcare: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario: A hospital deploys AutoGen for a multi-agent system to assist in oncology treatment planning. Agents include a symptom analyzer, a drug interaction checker, and a dosing calculator, all conversing to recommend chemotherapy regimens based on patient records. During a pilot, the system processes de-identified PHI from 50 patients, but enters an infinite loop debating a rare drug interaction, repeatedly referencing patient IDs in context. This exposes PHI to excessive LLM calls, generates unverified code for dosage calculations, and converges on an incorrect recommendation, risking patient harm.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: With Vex integrated as a reply filter, the loop is detected after 8 turns, terminating the conversation and logging an audit trail. PHI leakage is blocked in real-time, unsafe code execution is prevented, and all decisions are timestamped for FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance. The hospital avoids a potential $1.5M HIPAA fine, enables safe human-in-the-loop review, and deploys the system confidently, reducing diagnostic errors by 40%.
